item.title: Topolski's Chronicle, V.2 No.3, 1954
item.creator: Topolski, Feliks, 1907-1989
item.description: Topolski's Chronicle is a series of broadsheets created by Feliks Topolski and distributed bimonthly to subscribers from 1953 through 1982. Topolski was a superlative draughtsman and covered current events with an artistic, unique style.

pages.typeset_text:
UNIVERSITY OF UTAH LIBRARIES
TOPOLSKI'S CHRONICLE No.3 (15) Vol.II 1954
of a voyage round the world ©
NEW DELHI the capital of the New Republic
TOPOLSKI'S CHRONICLE IS PUBLISHED 25 TIMES A YEAR AT 2s 6d. A COPY.
YEARLY SUBSCRIPTION RATE (WITH FOUR POSTPAID, INDEX AND MAP PACK OF THE WORLD) 38s. 6d. UNITED STATES, CANADA, OTHER COUNTRIES PRO-RATA AIR MAIL QUOTATIONS.
SUBCRIPTIONS FROM OUTSIDE THE STERLING AREA MAY BE PAID THROUGH THE TIMESCO COUPON SCHEME
Subscriptions and Orders from shops and newsagents to The Times Publishing Company Limited, Printing House Square, London, E.C. 4. Telephone: City 4321. Telegrams: Times Newspapers, London.
pages.handwritten_text:
Dr. Prasad, the President, receives a group who read a chapter from the Koran
Dr. Sachdev, Chief of the State Probationary Children
Government House Chaprassis
Reception – by Dr. Bedi
Mr. Menon – Reception given to American news photographers
Maulana Azad
Dr. Rajendra Prasad at Prime Minister's House
Pandit Nehru
'At Home' given by the President
by F. Topolski
sketch_descriptions: A group seated in a formal setting, one individual reading from a book while others listen.
A smaller group of men in turbans and traditional dress, possibly Chaprassis (government messengers).
A crowd scene depicting a reception, with figures gathered at the entrance of a building.
A portrait of a woman identified as Dr. Sachdev, among others, engaged in conversation.
Two men in traditional garb, standing prominently at center.
A trio of Indian men in varying styles of dress, engaged in animated discussion.
An interior meeting scene with Dr. Prasad, depicted seated and receiving a group.
A portrait of an elderly man in a cap and glasses, identified as Maulana Azad.
A formal reception including Dr. Rajendra Prasad at Prime Minister Nehru's house, with conversations among bespectacled and traditionally dressed men.
A cluster of figures, some in Western suits and hats, others in Indian attire, interacting.
A uniformed officer and turbaned man observing a gathering.
Multiple smaller sketches of crowded scenes, emphasizing groups and public life.

events: At Home given by the President

pages.page_summary: This page from Topolski's Chronicle documents scenes and personalities from New Delhi in 1954, focusing on public receptions and prominent political figures such as President Rajendra Prasad, Prime Minister Nehru, and Maulana Azad. The expressive sketches capture both official events and the vibrant public life of India's new republic.

pages.typeset_text: NEW DELHI, THE CAPITAL OF THE NEW REPUBLIC 116 Luncheon at the Prime Minister's 117 The President's 'At Home' 118 Garden Party at Government House 119 Russian diplomat, traditionalist politician and Congress Party official 120 An Asian embassy reception 121 Prime Minister's 'At Home' 122, 123 Distinguished guests, Republic Day 124, 125, 126 Republic Day 127 The Representative of The Crown
pages.handwritten_text:
Garden Party at Government House
Burmese Ambass. [unclear]
Gyndia Ambass.
Russian Diplomat
New Delhi
11:25 AM The open air 'reception' in the bright sun, where they all sit round the circular arena, the sight of magnificently turbaned, cup-bearers [or] officials.
Russian Diplomat
sketch_descriptions: A group of officials and diplomats engaging in conversation at a garden party, with some figures labeled as ambassadors and a Russian diplomat.
Various guests, including women in traditional and formal attire, engaged in small group conversations at an outdoor event.
A crowd scene in New Delhi with tightly clustered figures, including individuals in turbans and officials talking amongst themselves.
A reception gathering with groups and pairs of guests in an open-air setting, some wearing official or ceremonial dress.
Portrait-focused vignettes showing individuals with distinct headwear, possibly dignitaries or officials, depicted in profile and three-quarter view.
A mingling crowd of distinguished guests, including a Russian diplomat, women in saris, and men in suits and turbans, during a Republic Day event.
pages.page_summary: This page captures a series of sketched scenes from official gatherings and celebrations in New Delhi during the early years of the Indian Republic, highlighting Republic Day, diplomatic receptions, and a garden party at Government House. Notable figures such as ambassadors and diplomats are featured amongst the diverse crowd.
